item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ran into this by accident and I absolutely loved it. This is definitely one of my favorites now.

item.title: Re: Too long for too little
item.content:encoded:
I agree. The lack of notes from our universe Foundation researchers on the files, and, as noted in the previous comment, the vagueness of special containment procedures, makes the treat and everything the SCP implies to not feel so important.
The idea is great, nevertheless.

item.title: Too long for too little
item.content:encoded:
Huh? So a group of junior employees voted out members of the 05 council and succeeded, and when dissolved it is is completely reformed by some other group and given back their full authority, while presumably in the middle of a Foundation civil war. During this, the Foundation is also able to control some/all of the governments all around the world if they like by inserting personnel to the highest halls of power on a whim.

An alternate reality skip made the Foundation kill everything in order to save everything from some other unknown thing, except instead the foundation just killed itself by attacking it's own locations randomly with skips, and then everybody dies. OR DID THEY?? Here's a poem.

Sorry, but -1. Considerable cleanup and condensation is required. Because of the very vague nature of it all, the payoff is just too small to justify reading things like a UIU report about how boring this SCP is. The general idea is solid but the execution is not.

Edit - I think putting the whole thing on one page would reduce the slog factor considerably even before removing any material. Additionally, clarity could easily be added by including further "mainline" SCP Foundation notes, particularly within the sub-articles and at the end of the entire thing. Proper researches wouldn't end their work with a poem about wolves.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One thing I found to be a bit vague: your special containment procedures. Given the nature of the foundation, these procedures need to be thorough. In these procedures, you say:

Personnel are to be screened for changes in behavior or personality after accessing these files.

And yet, there is no procedure given for if a change in behavior is detected. This one guy accessed the console, and he's been acting pretty weird since then. What do we do about that? Think about these containment procedures from the perspective of those that have to use them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First, technical:

  • As Shaggydredlocks said upthread, you probably really want to do this with ListPages. You will get one rating module and one discussion page for the whole thing which for articles in which the "supplements" aren't really supplemental is desirable.
  • The videos load with a sponsored ads now, which breaks immersion badly, IMO.

Secondly, I like this. It is similar to some other stuff here, but distinctive enough. Some of it is a bit of a slog, and I think maybe some of the purely technical stuff could be shorter, but none of that is enough of a detraction from this to change my vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Decided to go ahead and tapnek's input after a couple month's hiatus. Enjoy the improved viewing experience!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I fell in love with this the minute I read it. There's something unbelievably thrilling about this, and the images and GIFs you've used here make it all the more exciting. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this. It was a lot to read, and confusing for the first couple of pages, but for me that added to the payoff at the end. The log of special committee activities was brilliant, and getting to see the documentation of the Foundation destroying itself brought it home. IMO you earned all the sextuple upvotes.

By the time I got to the end, I had forgotten about the first entry and how this was information from another universe rather than a story about the destruction of our own. Moving that first page to the end, and putting the poem at the bottom of it, might make that part clearer ("whew, it was all a dream… or was it?"). But I'm not actually suggesting you change this, because imo it's a masterpiece as is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One of my new favorite SCPs! This was executed incredibly! Awesome job!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: About the videos, try using Vimeo like in SCP-3972 to embed them. Doesn't look too out of place that way. I would have suggested webms but Wikidot is so archaic that it still uses Flash to play mp3s.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for reading! In response to your questions and concerns: I decided on the GIF format because embedding them as Youtube videos would break immersion a little. I tried to compress the GIFs as much as possible to help alleviate the data problem, though.

The SSD was sent into the main SCP universe through a temporal anomaly related to the containment breaches. And yes, it's heavily implied that the entity is going from universe to universe, destroying everything as it goes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
About the skip itself, I'll echo what Scantron said in that it feels like a more subtle version of 3519, only instead of the Fifth Church causing everyone on the world to commit suicide thanks to their psycho religion, it's some outside force taking pleasure in having humanity eliminate itself from the face of the Earth. In other words, it also feels like a shorter version of Kalinin's Proposal.

Two questions though. How did one of those SSDs get sent to an alternate universe and is there a possibility that this outside entity would do it again with the main SCP universe?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: These GIF files are large and automatically loaded by the browser when the page is accessed. Do you think you can instead turn them into videos, upload them to YouTube or Vimeo, and embed them on the pages? Some of us don't have unlimited mobile data or have slow internet speeds.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I loved seeing this come together in the draft stages, and I love it now that it's now been posted. This is a great article and you should feel great for writing it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm so glad to see this holding up. It's outrageously ambitious and, in my opinion, does a beautiful job of the classic SCP formula— take something we find in everyday life (in this case authority) and turn it into something unusual. +1 on all pages, but you would have had that from me even in the version I gave feedback on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is horrible when you think of the perspectives of the researchers or the personnel; The persons that have the most power in the world, the persons that they're suppost to trust their entire existence to suddenly went bananas and wanted to nuke the Earth.

I'm in love with this. You get a +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel like I've seen this before — am I right in thinking that the infohazard's paranoia effect took over the Foundation higher-ups and accelerated them to ending the world? — but I still had a good time reading all the various things. I particularly like the addendum entitled "Help". :)

Also, it took me a second, but I'm assuming the "link doesn't match URL" skips in the final iteration are that way because this is coming from an alternate dimension?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My reading preference is 1958 -> 1960 -> 1961 -> 1962 -> 2038 -> our timeline. I think the twists are set up better that way, because the out-of-universe document 'spoils' how the anomaly works. It's not the ambiguity of the documents that's the problem, but the disambiguity of the first page.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yea this was pretty awesome start to finish - though with the number of iterations I feel like list pages would have been preferable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As far as I'm concerned, this accomplishes what SCP-3519 set out to do, with a lot more character, intrigue, and subtlety overall. The pacing issues, while real, were easy for me to overlook because I was just that interested in seeing how the piece would develop.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I pretty much agree with this to be honest.

I also have a few issues with your extra docs, most specifically that there are a lot of cases where you mention that the anomaly DOESN'T do something, presumably because it will begin doing it later. For example, in the 2nd (I think) you say that it doesn't affect humans, but I had no reason to believe it did in the first place.

Also, you call this an infohazard and I'm not sure that is what this is. Infohazards are linked to objects and manifest their effect when you refer to it in someway (writing about it, thinking about it etc). Pretty sure this is a meme (spreads to other images on contact) or a cognitohazard (infects humans who look at them), or both.

Aaand yeah, I felt there was very little pay off for this. I think the end was trying to suggest that the rocket, or the hard drive from it at least, was shunted to the baseline Foundation reality from this alternate one? I dunno. Especially for a piece this long I feel like this level of ambiguity is pretty bad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This. Pacing is the single biggest issue for an article that's otherwise super intriguing. It's not like 2998 where each part on its own adds mystery, intrigue, character, and forward-driving narrative to compel me. It just stalls in places, and that's what's gonna kill an article that is otherwise good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm sorry about all of the things you didn't like.

In answer to your main concern about unclear narrative and repetition: the documents are meant to be unclear and ambiguous. I thought it helped to introduce the possibility that the anomaly was actively modifying the pages. The final note is meant to add the idea that the anomaly was sent both back in time, creating itself, as well as being sent into our universe. However, because that note was written after the spacecraft was destroyed, there's no way we would have it over in our universe, right?

To put it simply, none of this is just raw, unmodified documentation. The anomaly has changed and added things, perhaps in an effort to explain how it came to be, perhaps to narcissistically play itself up. It's all close, but not quite clear intentionally.

I hope you reconsider your position.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Love this. The videos in particular are brilliant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, this was a lot to read, and thus, I have quite a few things to give feedback on.

For one, if you're going to post something that has a ton of ancillary materials, you should definitely have your links in place, and fill the articles in, one by one. I would also suggest announcing your SCP before you actually post it, that way people aren't seeing a bunch of random, and/or blanked, and/or strange articles show up with no context.

The biggest crit I can give this, and most over-arching is the lack of payoff for the amount of text there is here. There's a few different concepts here, none of which really warrant the amount of reading that took place.

First, your opener is pretty interesting. I'm a big fan of alternate universe skips, and i was looking forward to more.

From there though, you immediately drop in to an anomalous item document that says its not anomalous. Then it is anomalous. Then its ruining the O5 council, and nothing can stop us. Then its nukes? Then it's a K-class scenario? Then we gave the message to ourselves?

Maybe I'm not seeing the connections, but the actual anomaly here is the infohazard…which we sent to ourselves? Which was present in everything?

Basically, there's no focus. The narrative is unclear, and the writing, while…competent? Is mostly repetition and a lot of work for little payoff.

I also found the "note at the end" of the whole thing didn't really amount to much. From what I can gather, the infohazard forced the O5 council to release the skips as it were, yeah?

Either way. There was a LOT of work here, for not much payoff. I feel this could have been condensed in to a few sections of a shorter article, and had significantly better pacing.

Downvote in its current form (all six pages).

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
I came up with this while watching meme videos. No joke.

Thanks to Edward Buck, Modern_Erasmus, Pedantique, Waterfire, TheFuelTank, JamesTD, th4nkyoub3n, and MrWrong for the feedback. Special thanks to CannedBread for his incredibly in-depth critique, and to The Great Hippo for pretty much giving this story it's end.

All media used in this SCP is in the public domain. All of it is linked below:

+ Show
- Hide

Media Used in Photographs 1 and 2
Screen cap of the Operation Pulmbbob Test
Paper Texture
Media used in Diagram
Nuclear Symbol
EM radiation
Media used in Video 1
Footage of Operation Teapot
Hiroshima Burn Victim
"Naval Concepts of Chemical and Biological Warfare"
Media used in Video 2
Footage of Operation Dominic
Nazi Conncentration Camp Footage (This one's bad)
"Naval Concepts of Chemical and Biological Warfare"
Hurry Up
Media Used in Video 3
Pre-cut footage of Operation Teapot
Shiva Statue
Dr. Oppenheimer
Old Newspaper (used for lettering in several images
Media Used in Hidden Picture 1
"Is this tomorrow?" Propaganda Poster
Rust (used as a transparency to approximate degradation)
Media Used in Hidden Picture 2
Creepy Forest
Media used in Hidden Picture 3
ICBM launch
Proto-Shiva engraving
Fonts
Geo (SCiPNet)
Press Start (SCPNET)
Nixie One (AIA)

I'm sincerely sorry to anyone who has to actually verify all of this.
